content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Phaedra Norton 
City Attorney 
City of Turlock 
156 S. Broadway, Ste. 240 
Turlock, CA 95380 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-108 
Dear Ms. Norton: 
May 22, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the gift and conflict of interest 
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common 
law conflict of interest or Section l 090. Additionally, the Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby (1975) I 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and this letter is based on the facts presented. 
QUESTION 
If Turlock Mayor Gary Soiseth and Vice Mayor Amy Bublak participate in an upcoming 
skydiving event, are the cost of the flight, ground transportation, per diem, lodging and the value of 
the tandem jump a reportable gift, subject to the gift limit and conflict of interest disqualification? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The cost of the flight, ground transportation, per diem, lodging and the value of the 
tandem jump would be reportable on the Mayor and the Vice Mayor's Statements of Economic 
Interests as a gift, subject to gift limits and subject to conflict of interest disqualification for 12 
months. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I IO through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-108 
Page No. 2 
You are the Turlock City Attorney, and are requesting advice on behalf of Mayor Gary 
Soiseth and Vice Mayor Amy Bublak. The U.S. Army Recruiting Command has invited the Mayor 
Soiseth and Vice Mayor Bublak to participate in a tandem jump with the Golden Knights, at the 
Perris, California Airport. The flyer provides as follows: 
This event is underwritten by the U.S. Army Recruiting Command. 
Your flight arrangements will be made from Sacramento to Ontario Airports and 
return to Sacramento. (Mutually agreed upon time). Ground transportation will be 
provided by a designated driver in Ontario. You will receive per diem for meals and 
over-night lodging at Hampton Inn, 12611 Memorial Way Moreno Valley, CA 
92553 
According to information provided by the U.S. Army to the City, the value of the hotel 
accommodations provided is $ I 01.00 plus tax, the value of the per diem is roughly $42 a day ($84 
for two days) and the Army states that the jump itself has no cost or value as "it is an Army asset." 
For the purposes determining a value for the tandem jump, you have indicated that the cost of a 
tandem jump in Lodi is $ 100.00. The total value of this trip for each official is approximately $285. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's Gift Limit Provisio11s: 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as: 
. . .  any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or 
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in 
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official 
status. 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of 
gifts by local public officials in three ways: 
• Gift Limit of $470: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public 
officials. The current limit is $470 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; 
Regulation 18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all public officials. (Sections 89503(a) and 
87200.) 
• Gift Reporting: So that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the 
Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift (or any 
gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year 
are disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 87200 - 87210.) 


File No. A-17-108 
Page No. 3 
• 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from making, 
participating in making, or using his or her position to influence the outcome of a 
governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $4 70 
or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to 
the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e); Regulations 18700 and 
18703.4.) 
Regulation 18946 provides specific guidance for the of valuation of gifts. Regarding the 
valuation of unique gifts, Regulation l 8946(b) states: 
Whenever the fair market value of a gift cannot be determined because the gift is 
unique or unusual, the official must make a reasonable approximation. In making 
such an approximation, the official must take into account the price of similar 
items. If similar items are not available as a guide, a good faith estimate shall be 
utilized. 
Therefore, the cost of the flight, ground transportation, per diem, lodging and the value of 
the tandem jump are a reportable gift and subject to the gift limit of$470. Further, as noted above, a 
public official who receives gifts of $470 or more may have a financial conflict of interest under the 
Act. (Sections 87100 et seq.) However, it does not appear that the value of this trip will be $470 or 
more for either official. The gifts would be reportable. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
